name: Marian McPartland
description: British pianist, composer, writer and radio host (1918–2013)
abstract: Margaret Marian McPartland OBE (née Turner; 20 March 1918 – 20 August 2013), was an English and American jazz pianist, composer, and writer. She was the host of Marian McPartland's Piano Jazz on National Public Radio from 1978 to 2011.

quotes.text: A particular . . . Obviously we’ve got people like . . . We haven’t even mentioned, like Bird and Sonny Stitt and all those players who . . . I remember Bill Crow said to me once . . . He made a very good remark which I tried to remember. He said, “Piano players shouldn’t listen to all piano players. They should listen to horn players.” That’s really true, because . . . That fits right in with John Mehegan saying, “Marian, there’s not enough Bird in your playing.” You do get ideas and things, not to necessarily copy, but to give you ideas about what to play. There were tunes by Chick Corea. I learned to play “Windows.” I love that tune. A lot of things that he did before he had that electric band. He was with Stan Getz. I remember some interesting music that he did then. Of course I could listen to Stan Getz forever, as far as that’s concerned. I think Stan Getz may have turned me on to more beautiful tunes . . . like hearing him at Carnegie Hall play Billy Strayhorn’s tune “Blood Count.” I think the way he played that, I had to go right out and learn it, and all these other tunes, like “Isfahan.” I learned a lot of those tunes so that I could . . . I think actually for some strange reason I was one of the first people to put out an album of all Billy Strayhorn tunes. It was amazing. Art Farmer did one about the same time, and then the two of us played on a concert at Pace University, doing . . . Art doing his different selections of Billy Strayhorn tunes and me doing mine. I’m very proud of that album. We’re talking about . . . well, Wayne Shorter. There’s a guy I’ve listened to for years and still mystified by all those tunes. I can play “Footprints,” and there’s one or two other tunes I’d love . . . “Infant Eyes.” That’s a tune. Do you play that?
quotes.caption: Stan Getz playing 'Blood Count' at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Marian McPartland — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Michael Moore
about_quotes.evidence: he recommended me to Marian McPartland. And I played with Marian McPartland and she liked me and hired me... Marian and I talked about that recently, and three or four of my tunes were on there
about_quotes.doc_title: Michael Moore interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Tate
about_quotes.evidence: I was also working with Marian McPartland at that time... I came to New York working with Marian with her trio.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Tate interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 17, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Rufus Reid
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] I think it was Marian McPartland who said, via Alec Wilder, “I’d kill for a good bass line.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Rufus Reid interviewed by Monk Rowe, Atlanta, GA, January 4, 2013

about_quotes.speaker: Marty Morell
about_quotes.evidence: Yeah, yeah, back in the day, yeah I played with her. ... And I was working with Marian.
about_quotes.doc_title: Marty Morell interviewed by Monk Rowe, Orlando, Florida, February 24, 2017

about_quotes.speaker: Jake Hanna
about_quotes.evidence: I went out with Marian, right after that I went with her trio, for years.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jake Hanna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie Cain and Roy Kral
about_quotes.evidence: And so Friday Jackie’s going back and learning this song, that she’s going to do Saturday, with Marian. ... And so we ran through it once in the theater, you know, when we came in for the sound check.
